era_context: The Ming Dynasty is in the later years of the Yongle Emperor's reign (r. 1402–1424). The Hussite Wars are ongoing in Bohemia, with Jan Žižka still commanding Hussite forces. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power under Murad II (r. 1421–1451). The Portuguese are beginning systematic exploration of the African coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (no contemporary written records)
Northern and Eastern Europe outside major political centers

events.title: Yongle Emperor's Later Reign: Military and Cultural Achievements
events.description: In 1423, the Yongle Emperor was in the later years of his reign, which had been marked by significant military campaigns, the construction of the Forbidden City (completed 1420), and the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia (completed 1408). The emperor was also sponsoring Zheng He's treasure voyages, which continued to demonstrate Ming naval power and diplomatic reach. The Yongle Emperor's reign represented the height of Ming expansionism and cultural achievement.
events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Jan Žižka's Military Campaigns
events.description: In 1423, Jan Žižka was leading Hussite forces in military campaigns against Catholic armies and rival factions. The Hussite Wars, sparked by the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, were in their early and most radical phase. Žižka's innovative military tactics, particularly the use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg), were proving effective against larger Catholic armies. The year 1423 saw continued Hussite military success.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: Contemporary chronicles document Hussite military campaigns in detail.

events.title: Ottoman Expansion in the Balkans
events.description: Murad II, Ottoman Sultan since 1421, was consolidating Ottoman control over the Balkans in 1423. Ottoman forces were expanding their territorial holdings and suppressing internal rebellions. This period saw the gradual encirclement of Constantinople, which would fall in 1453. Ottoman military organization and gunpowder technology were advancing, making them a formidable power in the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Zheng He's Treasure Voyages Continue
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's treasure voyages continued in 1423 as part of the Yongle Emperor's program of maritime expansion and diplomatic outreach. These massive expeditions, involving hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, reached the coasts of East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean. The voyages demonstrated Ming naval supremacy and were intended to establish tributary relationships with distant states.

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingya Shenglan)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: The exact dates of Zheng He's voyages are debated by scholars. Contemporary documentation is limited; most records are from later compilations.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of the African Coast
events.description: Portuguese expeditions under Prince Henry the Navigator continued systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1423. These voyages were motivated by trade, gold, and religious conversion. By 1423, Portuguese knowledge of African geography was gradually expanding, though major breakthroughs would come later.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of early Portuguese exploration is uncertain. Most detailed accounts come from later chronicles.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The Yongle Emperor's reign has been reinterpreted by recent scholarship, which emphasizes the costs of his military campaigns and the eventual curtailment of his expansionist policies by his successors. The significance of Zheng He's voyages has been debated: while traditionally celebrated as demonstrations of Ming power, recent scholarship emphasizes their diplomatic and tributary functions rather than exploration for its own sake. Some scholars argue that the voyages were less significant than European exploration in terms of long-term global impact, though this reflects Eurocentric historiography. The Portuguese exploration narrative has been complicated by postcolonial scholarship.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1423 is similar to previous years: heavily weighted toward East Asia and Europe, with minimal coverage of Africa, the Americas, and other regions. Chinese sources are abundant but retrospective. European sources are contemporary but elite-focused. The 'significance' of events reflects historiographic bias toward literate, centralized states.

graph_edges.note: Yongle Emperor's reign ended with his death in 1424
Jan Žižka's campaigns continued until his death in 1424
Ottoman expansion in the Balkans was part of the process leading to the fall of Constantinople
Zheng He's voyages were part of a continuous program of Ming maritime expansion
